items.mistake: Using divisor n instead of n - 1 when calling it "sample covariance"
items.fix: Match your estimator to your library: unbiased sample covariance uses (n - 1), as in `np.cov` default and most textbooks.
items.why: Mixing conventions changes numeric values and breaks consistency with variance estimates and correlation formulas.
items.example: For x = [1, 2, 3], y = [2, 4, 6], n - 1 gives s_xy = 2; divisor n gives 4/3—same sign, different magnitude.

items.mistake: Computing covariance or correlation without centering the data
items.fix: Subtract column means (or use library functions that center internally) before applying the product-sum formula.
items.why: Raw cross-products Σ xᵢ yᵢ confound means with co-movement and are not Cov(X, Y).
items.example: If all yᵢ = 10, raw Σ xᵢ yᵢ scales with x̄ even when y is constant; centered products are zero.

items.mistake: Interpreting correlation near zero as "no relationship"
items.fix: Plot the data; test for non-linear patterns or use additional measures (Spearman, mutual information) when linearity is doubtful.
items.why: Pearson r only measures linear association; U-shaped or circular patterns can yield r ≈ 0.
items.example: Y = X² on symmetric X gives r ≈ 0 despite Y being fully determined by X.

items.mistake: Comparing raw covariances across features with different scales
items.fix: Use the correlation matrix or standardize features before comparing pairwise strengths.
items.why: A feature measured in millimeters may show tiny covariance with another while a dollar-denominated feature shows huge covariance purely from units.
items.example: Cov(height_cm, weight_kg) and Cov(height_m, weight_kg) differ by a factor of 100 in the height scale.
